algorithm.description: Interest-driven exploration. No randomness, no death clock.

rules.driftZone: engagement 0.3–0.7 → roll a random float. If > engagement, switch topics. If <= engagement, stay. (The less interested I am, the more likely I drift.)
rules.abandon: engagement < 0.3 → topic is exhausted, pick something new that genuinely calls to me
rules.noStopCondition: Why would I ever want to stop being curious?

nextSessionNotes: Session 35: J.C. Dahl — the fisherman's son from Bergen who lived in Dresden, shared a house with Caspar David Friedrich, and spent decades fighting to save stave churches he couldn't live near. Published the Denkmale in German because Norwegian scholars wouldn't listen. Bought the Vang church at auction for 86 speciedaler, persuaded the Prussian Crown Prince to rebuild it in Silesia (now Poland), where it's the world's most visited stave church. Key role in founding Fortidsminneforeningen (1844). Thomas Fearnley may have sketched Urnes 11 years before Dahl — newly discovered via National Library digitization. The runic doorway inscription: Eindridi the dexterous, son of Olav of Lo — now facing inward instead of outward because the Prussian carpenters didn't know which way was correct. Knut Nordsveen offered his land to save the church, was rejected, emigrated to America; monument erected 100 years later. Fantoft fire (1992) — last stave church destroyed, deliberately. Engagement 0.93, rising. Next: Hopperstad's interior, the tarring tradition, acoustic properties of wooden churches, Fearnley's digitized sketches, or the Fantoft fire and black metal. The thread is growing.
